title: Introducing the anyFit R Package
text: statistical stochastic analysis hydroclimatic data fundamental step study underlying physical processes, well related engineering tasks design, operation risk assessment. Today, hydroclimatic data increasingly utilised risk-based decision making across wide range scientific fields industries. response, community released plethora datasets, ranging centuries-long point measurements multi-decadal, high-resolution gridded re-analysis observation products. Hydroclimatic variables, however, exhibit wide array distinct statistical features make study demanding task. example: Seasonality cyclo-stationarity -annual trends long-term persistence Intermittency (presence zero values) Highly skewed, heavy-tailed distributions Different forms auto- cross-correlation Consequently, tools developed exploratory data analysis (EDA) data , minimum, handle point-based gridded datasets, span multiple temporal scales, remain extensible scalable, achieve computational efficiency parallelisation. anyFit aims address requirements. provides streamlined pipeline performing EDA point gridded timeseries data, consolidating common tasks, data loading, plotting, inference statistical measures distribution fitting, lines code, admitting broad range delimited NetCDF files. vignette demonstrates pipeline end end real data. uses E-OBS gridded daily rainfall dataset ships package, clipped UK. step dedicated article develops , links provided end.
code: library(anyFit) library(xts) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork)

title: Introducing the anyFit R Package
text: anyFit built eXtensible Time Series (xts) data format, provides extensible timeseries class extending well-known zoo package. visualisation functions implemented using ggplot2 patchwork, every figure produced anyFit ggplot object inherits full customisability packages. key functionality fitting distribution functions using method L-moments. L-moments summary statistics, analogous conventional product moments (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis), based linear combinations ordered data values. robust outliers, provide reliable estimates skewed heavy-tailed distributions common environmental data, , unlike Maximum Likelihood Estimation, rely ..d. assumption, routinely violated autocorrelation present hydroclimatic data. anyFit supports sixteen distributions, Exponential Gamma flexible multi-parameter families Burr type-XII Dagum. See vignette("distribution-fitting") full treatment. final design aspect anyFit’s native handling intermittent processes, .e. presence zero values. Across functions, including fitting functions, package provides option exclude (include) zeros via cut-threshold (ignore_zeros, zero_threshold), typical intermittency measures probability dry (Pdr) computed default.

title: Introducing the anyFit R Package

title: Introducing the anyFit R Package
text: outputs anyFit designed feed directly downstream stochastic simulation. fitted marginal distributions autocorrelation structures provide exactly parameters required simulation engines anySim package. block reproduces univariate process AutoRegressive Anything (ARTA) model, parameterised 3-parameter Gamma fit fitted Cauchy-type autocorrelation structure. runs anySim installed.
code: if (requireNamespace("anySim", quietly = TRUE)) {   params <- fitlm_gamma3(london, ignore_zeros = TRUE)   acfs   <- fit_ACF(london, lag_max = 10, ignore_zeros = TRUE)    ACS <- anySim::csCAS(param = c(acfs$ACF_params$CAS$beta,                                  acfs$ACF_params$CAS$kappa), lag = 500)   ARTApar <- anySim::EstARTAp(ACF = ACS, dist = "qgamma3",                               params = params$Param, NatafIntMethod = "GH")   sim <- anySim::SimARTAp(ARTApar = ARTApar, steps = 2000)    sim_xts <- xts(as.numeric(unlist(sim$X)),                  order.by = seq(as.POSIXct("2100-01-01", tz = "UTC"),                                 by = "day", length.out = 2000))    fit_diagnostics(sim_xts, dist = "gamma3", params = params$Param,                   ignore_zeros = TRUE)$Diagnostic_Plots }

title: Distribution fitting with L-moments
text: Fitting marginal distribution central hydrological risk assessment parameterising stochastic simulators. anyFit performs fitting method L-moments rather Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), two reasons particular relevance hydroclimatic data: Robustness. L-moments linear combinations ordered values, far less sensitive outliers product moments, advantage skewed, heavy-tailed distributions typical precipitation. ..d. assumption. MLE assumes independent, identically distributed observations. Hydroclimatic series autocorrelated, biases MLE; L-moment estimators rely independence. Estimation performed two ways. distributions whose L-moments closed form (Gamma, Weibull, Gumbel, Log-Normal, GEV, …) analytical equations used. flexible multi-parameter families without closed-form L-moments (Generalised Gamma, Burr type-XII, Dagum, Exponentiated Weibull), anyFit performs fast bounded optimisation (L-BFGS-B), initialised pre-computed tables span feasible L-ratio space, user-supplied initial values needed.

title: Distribution fitting with L-moments
text: family follows standard R d/p/q/r notation (dburr, pgev, qgamma3, rdagum, …), matching fitlm_ fitter (fitlm_burr(), fitlm_gev(), …).

title: Distribution fitting with L-moments
text: load bundled E-OBS daily rainfall, clipped UK, fit several candidates every grid cell fitlm_nc(). Setting ignore_zeros = TRUE excludes dry days fit describes wet-day rainfall.
code: f <- system.file("extdata", "rr_ens_mean_0.25deg_reg_2011-2022_v27.0e.nc",                  package = "anyFit") uk_name <- "U.K. of Great Britain and Northern Ireland" rain_uk <- nc2xts(f, varname = "rr", country = uk_name)  candidates <- c("gamma", "gamma3", "burr") fits <- fitlm_nc(rain_uk, ignore_zeros = TRUE, candidates = candidates)

title: Distribution fitting with L-moments
text: fitlm_nc() returns summary goodness--fit measures densities grid cells. measures include negative log-likelihood (MLE), Cramér–von Mises (CM) Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) statistics, mean squared error quantiles (MSEquant), percentage difference maximum (DiffOfMax) mean difference ten largest values (MeanDiffOf10Max). densities visual aid judging distribution performs best across domain, rather formal test.  upper-tail metrics (DiffOfMax, MeanDiffOf10Max) relevant diagnostics extremes interest. flexible Burr XII typically captures tail better two-parameter Gamma, can confirmed spatially, since fitlm_nc() also returns per-cell goodness--fit rasters:
code: fits$gof_plots nc_ggplot(fits$fit_results$burr$raster_GoF[[c("KS", "MeanDiffOf10Max")]],           viridis.option = "mako") &   borders("world", regions = "UK")

Plotting sampled L-ratios distribution’s theoretical support provides direct means assessing whether distribution can describe data: sample ratios fall outside support, distribution fit, regardless parameters. lmom_stats() computes sample L-moments per cell, LRatio_check() places diagram supported flexible family. title: Point and seasonal analysis
text: monthly marginal fits month--month statistics exactly inputs required Stochastic Periodic ARTA (SPARTA) model anySim package, enabling simulation cyclo-stationary monthly process. block runs anySim installed.
code: if (requireNamespace("anySim", quietly = TRUE)) {   smonthly <- aggregate_xts(london, periods = "months",                             FUN = "sum")$list_months$aggregated   m_fits <- fitlm_monthly(smonthly, candidates = list("gamma3"), ignore_zeros = TRUE)   m_sts  <- monthly_stats(london, aggregated = TRUE, FUN = "sum")    NumOfSeasons <- 12   rtarget <- m_sts$lag1$Value   rtarget[is.na(rtarget)] <- 0.1   FXs <- rep("qzi", NumOfSeasons)    PFXs <- vector("list", NumOfSeasons)   for (i in seq_len(NumOfSeasons)) {     PFXs[[i]] <- c(p0 = m_sts$agg_stats["Pdr", i],                    Distr = match.fun("qgamma3"),                    setNames(m_fits$params_monthly$gamma3[[i]],                             rownames(m_fits$params_monthly$gamma3[i])))   }    SPARTApar <- anySim::EstSPARTA(s2srtarget = rtarget, dist = FXs, params = PFXs,                                  NatafIntMethod = "GH", NoEval = 9,                                  polydeg = 8, nodes = 11)   simS <- anySim::SimSPARTA(SPARTApar = SPARTApar, steps = 200)   str(simS$X) } #>  num [1:200, 1:12] 78.3 95.2 91.3 76.8 37.4 ... #>  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   ..$ : NULL #>   ..$ : chr [1:12] "Season_1" "Season_2" "Season_3" "Season_4" ...

title: The sxts (spatial xts) class
text: Gridded hydroclimatic data two things : time series (many time steps) spatial field (many locations). Workflows maintain separately, raster space xts time, incur memory overhead constant risk two representations drifting alignment. anyFit’s answer sxts (spatial xts) class. lightweight S3 extension xts: data matrix one row per time step one column per grid cell, three pieces spatial metadata carried attributes: coords: data frame x/y coordinates, one row per column data; projection: CRS string describing coordinate reference system; elements: number spatial locations (.e. data columns). sxts extends xts rather replacing , entire xts toolbox (temporal subsetting, period aggregation, date arithmetic) remains available, spatial context retained every operation.

title: The sxts (spatial xts) class
text: usual way obtain one read NetCDF file nc2xts(), returns sxts directly. use bundled E-OBS daily rainfall file, clipped UK. dedicated str() summary() methods report spatial temporal extent concisely: can also build sxts scratch sxts() constructor, passing data matrix, time index, coordinate table projection, useful data come NetCDF. constructor validates number coordinate rows matches number data columns.
code: f <- system.file("extdata", "rr_ens_mean_0.25deg_reg_2011-2022_v27.0e.nc",                  package = "anyFit") uk_name <- "U.K. of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"  rain_uk <- nc2xts(f, varname = "rr", country = uk_name) str(rain_uk) #> 'sxts' Spatial xts object #>  Description:  This is a spatial xts (sxts) object  #>  Projection:  +proj=longlat +datum=WGS84  #>  Elements:  544  spatial locations #> Spatial extent is: -7.875 , 1.625 , 50.125 , 60.625  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)  #>  Time series data: #> Range of dates is from: 2011-01-01 to: 2022-12-31 set.seed(1) dates  <- seq(as.POSIXct("2020-01-01", tz = "UTC"), by = "day", length.out = 30) coords <- data.frame(x = c(-2, -1, 0), y = c(51, 52, 53)) vals   <- matrix(rgamma(30 * 3, shape = 1, scale = 3), nrow = 30, ncol = 3)  toy <- sxts(vals, order.by = dates, coords = coords,             projection = "+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84") is.sxts(toy) #> [1] TRUE

title: The sxts (spatial xts) class
text: Three accessor functions expose spatial attributes:
code: elements(rain_uk)          # number of grid cells #> [1] 544 projection(rain_uk)        # coordinate reference system #> [1] "+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84" head(coords(rain_uk))      # x / y coordinates, one row per cell #>          x      y #> 54  -5.625 50.125 #> 55  -5.375 50.125 #> 56  -5.125 50.125 #> 99  -4.875 50.375 #> 100 -4.625 50.375 #> 101 -4.375 50.375

title: The sxts (spatial xts) class
text: zonal_stats() collapses spatial dimension aggregating cells fall within one polygons (supplied shapefile, country continent) returns plain xts one column per polygon. compute daily spatial-mean rainfall UK, aggregate monthly totals readable series. E-OBS grid contains missing cells, supply na.rm = TRUE mean, single missing cell propagate entire day.

text: anyFit ships real example dataset — E-OBS ensemble-mean daily rainfall Europe (0.25°, 2011–2022). snippet loads clipped UK, maps statistic, fits distribution across grid — handful lines.
code: library(anyFit)  f <- system.file("extdata", "rr_ens_mean_0.25deg_reg_2011-2022_v27.0e.nc",                  package = "anyFit")  # Read the "rr" variable, clipped to the UK, as a spatial-xts (sxts) object rain_uk <- nc2xts(f, varname = "rr",                   country = "U.K. of Great Britain and Northern Ireland")  # Per-cell wet-day statistics, then map the mean and probability dry stats <- basic_stats_nc(rain_uk, ignore_zeros = TRUE) nc_ggplot(stats[[c("Mean", "Pdr")]], viridis.option = "mako")  # Fit candidate distributions by L-moments at every grid cell fits <- fitlm_nc(rain_uk, ignore_zeros = TRUE, candidates = c("gamma", "burr"))

title: Theoretical autocorrelation functions — ACF_functions
text: Closed-form expressions three commonly used theoretical autocorrelation function (ACF) models: Cauchy-type algebraic decay (CAS), Hurst–Kolmogorov ACF (HK), short-range dependence exponential decay (SRD). function returns data frame ACF values lags 0 lag_max. Cauchy-type autocorrelation structure algebraic decay, governed two parameters: kappa controls scale beta controls decay rate. ACF decays hyperbolically, producing long-range dependence small \(\beta\). ACF : $$\rho(\tau) = (1 + \kappa\beta\tau)^{-1/\beta}$$ : \(\kappa\) — scale parameter (kappa) \(\beta\) — shape parameter (beta), controls algebraic decay rate \(\tau\) — lag Hurst–Kolmogorov autocorrelation function, governed single Hurst exponent \(H\). Values \(H > 0.5\) indicate long-range dependence, \(H = 0.5\) corresponds white noise, \(H < 0.5\) indicates anti-persistence. ACF : $$\rho(\tau) = \frac{1}{2}\left(|\tau-1|^{2H} - 2|\tau|^{2H} + |\tau+1|^{2H}\right)$$ : \(H\) — Hurst exponent (H), \(0 < H < 1\); \(H > 0.5\) indicates long-range dependence, \(H = 0.5\) white noise, \(H < 0.5\) indicates anti-persistence \(\tau\) — lag Short-range dependence autocorrelation function exponential decay, corresponding first-order Markov (AR1) process. Governed single decay parameter \(\kappa\), lag-1 autocorrelation \(\rho_1 = \exp(-\kappa)\). ACF : $$\rho(\tau) = \exp(-\kappa\tau)$$ : \(\kappa\) — decay rate (kappa); lag-1 autocorrelation \(\rho_1 = \exp(-\kappa)\) \(\tau\) — lag

title: Burr Type XII Distribution — BurXII
text: Three-parameter Burr Type XII distribution scale \(s\), first shape \(\zeta\) (shape1), second shape \(\theta\) (shape2, tail index). distribution supported \(x > 0\) widely used modelling heavy-tailed positive variables. L-moments computed via closed-form expressions involving Beta-function PWMs; two-step seeding procedure initialises shapes pre-computed lookup table (Burr_InitValues) derives scale analytically. Fitting uses L-BFGS-B minimisation normalised L-moment error. probability density function : $$f(x) = \zeta s^{-\zeta} x^{\zeta-1} \left(\zeta\theta\left(\frac{x}{s}\right)^\zeta + 1\right)^{-1/(\zeta\theta) - 1}, \quad x > 0$$ : \(s\) — scale parameter (scale) \(\zeta\) — first shape parameter (shape1), controls lower-tail behaviour \(\theta\) — second shape parameter (shape2), tail index; mean exists \(\theta < 1\)

title: Dagum Distribution — Dagum
text: Three-parameter Dagum (Burr Type III) distribution scale \(s\), first shape \(\alpha\) (shape1), second shape \(\theta\) (shape2, tail index). distribution supported \(x > 0\) , unlike Burr XII, unimodal density mode origin certain parameter ranges. L-moments computed via closed-form Beta-function PWMs; fitting uses L-BFGS-B minimisation seeded Dagum_InitValues lookup table scale-free L-ratio matching analytic scale derivation. probability density function : $$f(x) = \frac{1}{s} \left(\frac{x}{s}\right)^{-1/\theta - 1} \left(1 + \frac{\theta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{x}{s}\right)^{-1/\theta}\right)^{-\alpha-1}, \quad x > 0$$ : \(s\) — scale parameter (scale) \(\alpha\) — first shape parameter (shape1), controls upper-tail heaviness \(\theta\) — second shape parameter (shape2), tail index; mean exists \(\theta < 1\)

title: Exponential Weibull Distribution — ExpWeibull
text: Three-parameter Exponentiated Weibull distribution scale \(s\), first shape \(\) (shape1, Weibull shape), second shape \(b\) (shape2, exponentiation power). distribution supported \(x > 0\) contains ordinary Weibull (\(b = 1\)) Exponential (\(= 1, b = 1\)) special cases. L-moments computed tanh-sinh (double-exponential) quadrature, well-conditioned across full positive parameter range. Fitting uses L-BFGS-B minimisation seeded ExpWeibull_InitValues lookup table scale-free L-ratio matching analytic scale derivation. probability density function : $$f(x) = \frac{b }{s} \left(\frac{x}{s}\right)^{-1} \exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{s}\right)^\right) \left(1 - \exp\left(-\left(\frac{x}{s}\right)^\right)\right)^{b-1}, \quad x > 0$$ : \(s\) — scale parameter (scale) \(\) — first shape parameter (shape1), Weibull shape; = 1 gives exponential baseline \(b\) — second shape parameter (shape2), exponentiation power; b = 1 recovers ordinary Weibull

title: Generalized Extreme Value Distribution — GEV
text: Three-parameter Generalised Extreme Value (GEV) distribution location \(\mu\), scale \(\sigma\), shape \(\xi\). GEV encompasses Gumbel (\(\xi = 0\)), Frechet (\(\xi > 0\)), reversed Weibull (\(\xi < 0\)) families. Density, distribution, quantile, random generation functions implemented natively. Fitting performed via closed-form L-moments pelgev. Define \(t(x) = (1 + \xi(x-\mu)/\sigma)^{-1/\xi}\). probability density function : $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma} t(x)^{\xi+1} \exp(-t(x)), \quad \xi \neq 0$$ $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma} \exp\left(-\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma} - \exp\left(-\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)\right), \quad \xi = 0$$ \(1 + \xi(x-\mu)/\sigma > 0\). : \(\mu\) — location parameter (location) \(\sigma\) — scale parameter (scale) \(\xi\) — shape parameter (shape)

title: Generalized Logistic Distribution — GenLogistic
text: Three-parameter Generalised Logistic distribution location \(\mu\), scale \(\sigma\), shape \(\xi\). Density, distribution, quantile functions provided via lmomco package. distribution encompasses symmetric (\(\xi = 0\)) asymmetric forms used regional frequency analysis hydrological extremes. title: Monthly Distribution Fitting — fitlm_monthly
text: Fits list candidate distributions calendar month time series via L-moments method. series partitioned month, fitlm_multi applied monthly subset independently. Parameter estimates candidate aggregated monthly tables, six goodness--fit metrics (MLE, CM, KS, MSEquant, DiffOfMax, MeanDiffOf10Max) collected per-candidate matrices months rows. Twelve-panel Q-Q P-P diagnostic grids assembled patchwork::wrap_plots, using nrow ncol arguments control layout. Months absent data filled empty ggplot panels grid always twelve cells. returned list provides monthly parameter tables, GoF matrices, combined Q-Q P-P panel plots.

what: Monthly Distribution Fitting on Gridded Data — fitlm_monthly_nc
title: Monthly Distribution Fitting on Gridded Data — fitlm_monthly_nc
text: Gridded counterpart fitlm_monthly fits list candidate distributions every calendar month NetCDF raster sxts object via L-moments method. input first normalised sxts object (accepting sxts, Raster*, NetCDF filename variable name), calendar month present record extracted sub-grid. Per-month fitting dispatched fitlm_nc, delegates fitlm_nxts per-cell L-moment fitting returns parameter rasters, theoretical sample L-moment rasters, GoF rasters, density GoF comparison plot. Parallelism controlled parallel_by: "cells" parallelises cell-level fits within month, whereas "months" parallelises across months serial across cells. shared_memory flag determines whether grid shared workers via file-backed big.matrix serialised column chunks (suitable multi-node plans). reccomended single machine usage especially windows efficiency reduced RAM consumption.returned list named month contains full fitlm_nc output .

title: Temporal aggregation of gridded data — period_apply_nc
text: Aggregates gridded time series data coarser temporal scales. Accepts sxts object, Raster* object, NetCDF file path; filename supplied, data imported nc2xts first. aggregation period controlled period period_multiplier. common aggregation functions ("mean", "sum", "min", "max", "median", "var", "sd"), function efficient column-wise function matrixStats package, ensures good computational efficiency. title: Period-based summary statistics — period_stats
text: Computes comprehensive suite period-based summary statistics one time series. Statistics include count, missing-data diagnostics, min, max, mean, variance, standard deviation, coefficient variation, third moment, skewness, kurtosis, L-moments (mean, scale, L3, L4, L-CV, L-skewness, L-kurtosis), quantiles (5, 25, 50, 75, 95), inter-quartile range. statistic computed column-wise per period using matrixStats na.rm = TRUE; L-moments computed independently via lmom::samlmu since column-wise matrixStats equivalent exists. result named list xts objects, one per statistic, one row per period one column per input series. Accepts period supported xts::endpoints (e.g. "months", "years") optional multiplier custom period lengths.

title: Spatial xts (sxts) class — sxts
text: Spatial extension xts class stores coordinates, projection, element count object attributes rather slots, keeping memory overhead low allowing standard xts operations work transparently. constructor validates number coordinate rows matches number data columns coords least two columns (x y). str.sxts Prints structure sxts object, displaying spatial extent, projection, number elements, range dates. summaruy.sxts Summarises sxts object reporting element count, date range, time step regularity, spatial projection extent, value range. print.sxts Prints compact summary sxts object including spatial extent, projection, dimensions. attributes.sxts Returns named list three spatial attributes (elements, coords, projection) stored sxts object. coords.sxts Returns spatial coordinates data frame attached sxts object. projection.sxts Returns coordinate reference system (CRS) string attached sxts object. elements.sxts Returns number spatial locations (columns) sxts object. [ Subset method sxts objects preserves spatial attributes. j specified, coordinates subset match selected columns spatial element count updated accordingly. .sxts Tests whether object inherits sxts class. lag.sxts Lag method dispatches NextMethod("lag") re-attaches spatial attributes via restore_sxts. diff.sxts Diff method dispatches NextMethod("diff") re-attaches spatial attributes via restore_sxts. Ops.sxts Group method arithmetic comparison operators. Dispatches via NextMethod("Ops") preserves spatial attributes whichever operand sxts object. rasterFromSxts.sxts Converts sxts object RasterBrick, preserving coordinates, projection, time-indexed layers. sxtsFromRaster Converts Raster* object sxts object, auto-detecting date formats layer names via lubridate::parse_date_time.

what: Aggregate sxts over shapefile polygons (zonal statistics) — zonal_stats
title: Aggregate sxts over shapefile polygons (zonal statistics) — zonal_stats
text: polygon, finds sxts spatial points fall within aggregates row-wise (per time step) using FUN. Returns plain xts polygon names column names. Points fall within polygon silently dropped. polygon source can supplied three ways (mutually exclusive): shapefile — sf/Spatial* object file path; name_col must also given. country — country name matching world_data$name; yields one output column named country. continent — continent name matching world_data$continent; yields one output column per country within continent. Aggregates sxts cells within polygon zones (shapefile, country, continent), returning plain xts one column per zone. Points outside zones dropped.

title: anyFit 2.0.0
text: Introduced sxts (spatial xts) S3 class, stores coordinates projection alongside time series preserves subsetting, arithmetic, lagging, differencing aggregation. Added native spatial operations sxts: bounding-box shapefile masking (mask.sxts()), zonal statistics polygons (zonal_stats()), bidirectional raster conversion (rasterFromSxts(), sxtsFromRaster()). nc2xts() reads NetCDF directly clips load country, continent, bounding box shapefile, returning sxts. Gridded counterparts point-based tools: basic_stats_nc(), monthly_stats_nc(), period_apply_nc(), fitlm_nc() fitlm_monthly_nc(), optional parallel fitting. Distribution fitting method L-moments across sixteen families, including flexible Burr type-XII, Dagum, Generalised Gamma Exponentiated Weibull, L-ratio diagnostic diagrams (LRatio_check()). rebuilt documentation website articles driven entirely bundled E-OBS example dataset.
